title: Reranking (LLM-based and Cross-Encoder)
one_liner: Re-score an oversized candidate set from vector search with a stronger relevance model (GPT-4o judge or a cross-encoder) and keep only the top few documents before generation.
problem_solved: Embedding-based (bi-encoder) similarity search often ranks superficially similar chunks above chunks that actually answer the question, because query and document are embedded independently and compared with a coarse cosine score. Reranking adds a second-pass scorer that examines each query-document pair jointly, so the final top-k passed to the LLM is precision-optimized rather than recall-optimized, cutting noise from the context window.
failure_modes: My retriever returns chunks that mention the query's keywords but don't actually answer the question
Irrelevant or marginally relevant chunks pollute the context window and dilute the answer
The correct passage is retrieved but sits at rank 8-20, below the k I pass to the LLM
Answers degrade because the generator is fed near-duplicate or vague chunks instead of the one that contains the fact
Embedding similarity can't distinguish nuanced intent (e.g. 'what is the capital' vs sentences praising the capital without naming it)
I want to retrieve broadly (high recall) but only send a few high-precision chunks to the LLM
when_to_use: Recall looks fine at k=20-30 but the top 3-5 results are poor - the answer is in the candidate set, just badly ordered
Your corpus has many near-duplicate or topically overlapping chunks and the generator needs the few that actually contain the answer
Queries require nuanced intent matching that cosine similarity misses (paraphrases, implicit answers, negations)
You want to shrink the context window (cost/token limits) without losing the relevant chunk
You already have a working vector-store RAG pipeline and want a drop-in quality upgrade without re-indexing

Choose the cross-encoder variant when you need low cost per query and can host a small model; choose the LLM-judge variant when you need instruction-aware relevance judgments and can afford per-document LLM calls
tradeoffs: LLM-based reranking makes one LLM call per candidate document (notebook: up to 30 GPT-4o calls per query) - expensive and slow; scores are also LLM-parse-dependent (notebook defaults to 0 on parse failure)

Cross-encoder reranking is far cheaper per pair but requires hosting/loading a sentence-transformers model and still adds a forward pass per candidate; it cannot follow instructions or use world knowledge like an LLM judge
Adds latency on every query (serial LLM scoring in the notebook is not batched or parallelized) - avoid for latency-critical interactive search
Cannot fix recall: if the answer chunk is not in the initial k candidates, no reranker will surface it - fix retrieval (fusion, query transformation) first
Overkill for small or clean corpora where top-k similarity search is already precise
Tuning two knobs (initial k and rerank_top_k) adds evaluation burden; too small an initial k silently caps quality
how_it_works: A standard pipeline (PDF -> encode_pdf helper -> FAISS vector store) performs initial retrieval with an intentionally large k (10-30 candidates via vectorstore.similarity_search). Two reranking implementations are then shown, each wrapped as a custom LangChain BaseRetriever so it slots directly into RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(chain_type='stuff'). Method 1 (LLM judge): a PromptTemplate asks GPT-4o (temperature=0) to rate each document's relevance to the query on a 1-10 scale, with llm.with_structured_output(RatingScore) - a Pydantic model with a relevance_score float - enforcing parseable scores; documents are scored one-by-one in a loop, sorted descending, and the top_n (2-3) are returned. Method 2 (cross-encoder): sentence_transformers CrossEncoder('cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2') jointly encodes [query, doc] pairs and predicts relevance scores in a single predict call over all pairs; a CrossEncoderRetriever retrieves k=10 candidates and returns rerank_top_k=5. A toy 'capital of France' comparison demonstrates the payoff: baseline similarity search returns keyword-similar filler sentences, while the reranked retriever surfaces the passages that actually name Paris. The technique operates purely post-retrieval; retrieval and generation stages are unchanged.
key_code_signals: llm.with_structured_output(RatingScore) where RatingScore is a Pydantic model with relevance_score: float
PromptTemplate asking GPT-4o to 'rate the relevance of the following document to the query' on a 1-10 scale
vectorstore.similarity_search(query, k=15/30) - over-retrieve then rerank_documents(query, docs, top_n=3)
class CustomRetriever(BaseRetriever, BaseModel) wrapping rerank inside get_relevant_documents
cross_encoder.predict([[query, doc.page_content], ...]) then sorted(zip(docs, scores), reverse=True)[:rerank_top_k]
related_techniques: reranking_with_llamaindex: alternative implementation of the same technique using LlamaIndex
fusion_retrieval: composes - hybrid BM25+vector retrieval produces the candidate set that reranking then reorders
contextual_compression: alternative post-retrieval cleanup - compresses/filters chunk content instead of reordering whole chunks
multi_faceted_filtering: alternative/complementary post-retrieval filtering by metadata and scores
simple_rag: prerequisite - baseline vector-store pipeline that reranking bolts onto
query_transformations: complementary - fixes recall at the query side while reranking fixes precision after retrieval
hierarchical_indices: complementary - improves candidate generation before the rerank stage
graphrag_with_milvus_vectordb: composes - uses reranking after multi-way graph+vector retrieval
retrieval_with_feedback_loop: complementary - adjusts relevance over time while reranking scores per-query
summary: Reranking adds a second-pass relevance scoring stage after initial vector retrieval: fetch a deliberately oversized candidate set (k=10-30) with cheap embedding similarity, then re-score each query-document pair with a stronger model and keep only the top few for generation. The notebook implements two rerankers as drop-in LangChain BaseRetriever subclasses feeding a RetrievalQA stuff chain: (1) an LLM judge where GPT-4o rates each document's relevance 1-10 via a prompt with Pydantic structured output, and (2) a local cross-encoder (cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 from sentence-transformers) that jointly encodes query+document pairs and scores them in one predict call. It fixes the classic bi-encoder failure where superficially similar chunks ('The capital of France is great') outrank chunks that actually answer the question ('...Paris...'), reducing context-window noise and improving grounding. Costs: per-query latency, one LLM call per candidate for the LLM variant, or model-hosting for the cross-encoder. Use when recall is fine but top-k precision is poor; it cannot recover documents the retriever never fetched.
notes_for_chunking: Structure is clean and section-driven: an H1 overview cell (Overview/Motivation/Key Components/Method Details/Benefits/Conclusion as H2s within one markdown cell - self-contained and highly embeddable on its own), then two clearly separated method sections ('## Method 1: LLM based function...' and '## Method 2: Cross Encoder models') each with '###' subsection headers preceding every code cell. Markdown/code ratio is roughly 50/50 with short, purposeful code cells. Junk to strip: the promotional book-ad cell at the very top (cell-0), pip install cell, git-clone + sys.path Colab-compat cell, duplicated wget download lines, three HTML cells referencing local SVGs, and a tracking-pixel markdown image at the end. The wildcard 'from helper_functions import *' hides encode_pdf/FAISS/OpenAIEmbeddings definitions - chunker should note external helper dependence. One valuable output: the 'capital of France' baseline-vs-reranked comparison output demonstrates the technique's payoff and is worth keeping with its code cell; other outputs are absent or trivial. Method 1 and Method 2 sections are independently self-contained and could be separate chunks; keep the RatingScore class with rerank_documents (same cell) and CrossEncoder instantiation with CrossEncoderRetriever (same cell).
